title: Assembled Materials: Polyelectrolyte-Surfactant Complexes
abstract:
Assembled Materials: Polyelectrolyte±
Surfactant Complexes**
By Shuiqin Zhou and Benjamin Chu*
1. Introduction
Interactions between polyelectrolytes and oppositely
charged amphiphiles have attracted a great deal of interest
in the last two decades, due to their importance both in fun-
damental polymer physics/biophysics and in biological and
industrial applications.[1±8] Developments on the formation
of polyelectrolyte±surfactant complexes have been de-
scribed elsewhere.[1,3] The interactions between polyelec-
trolytes and oppositely charged surfactants are quite strong
and can induce complex formation, often resulting in highly
ordered structures. Both electrostatic interactions between
the charged components and hydrophobic interactions be-
tween the polymer backbone and the alkyl chains of the
surfactant are important in driving the self-assembly of
(surfactant) molecules to form ordered structures. These
well-defined supramolecular structures of the polyelectro-
lyte±surfactant complexes have unusual mechanical, opti-
cal, electrical, and biological properties, which could be
very useful in the medical, cosmetic, food, painting, coat-
ing, enhanced oil recovery, and other technologies.
There are widespread methods for producing ordered
polymeric materials. However, the complexation of poly-
electrolytes with oppositely charged surfactants through
the self-assembly process offers a number of advantages:
they are easy to ªsynthesizeº and to isolate; and they have
controllable organization for the construction of a wide
variety of ordered structures. Furthermore, complex forma-
tion is often reversible with supramolecular structures that
can be rearranged in response to changes in the environ-
ment. By tuning the charge density, the flexibility and the
hydrophobicity of the polyelectrolyte chains as well as the
properties of surfactants (e.g., polar group, alkyl chains, po-
lar-to-non-polar volume ratio), the assembly behavior of
both polyelectrolyte chains and surfactant molecules may
be changed. Thus, it is possible to manipulate the supramo-
lecular structures on a nanoscopic scale and to design spe-
cific functions of polyelectrolyte±surfactant complexes.
A few comprehensive reviews on polyelectrolyte±surfac-
tant complexes have been published,[1±7] covering com-
plexes at interfaces, in non-aqueous solvents, and in the sol-
id state, with topics ranging from potential synthetic routes
to assembled structures and possible applications. Layered
structures are the predominant molecular arrangement.
Complexes formed by natural polyelectrolytes with oppo-
sitely charged surfactants have been studied long ago. Thal-
berg and Lindman[9] discussed complex formation between
polysaccharides and oppositely charged surfactants and
exploited their application in the purification of polysac-
charides. Herslof et al.[10] studied the salt effects on the in-
teraction between polysaccharides and oppositely charged
surfactants. The nature of protein±surfactant interactions
involved in complex formation in solution,[9] at solid sur-
faces,[11] at oil/water interfaces,[12] and in the solid state[7]
have been summarized.
In gene therapy, the low transfection efficiency of cation-
ic amphiphile-mediated gene delivery systems has limited
many practical applications for treatment of both acquired
and inherited diseases. DNA±surfactant complexes are un-
der intense investigation due to their importance in clarify-
ing the mechanisms of DNA delivery. Reviews on the com-
plexes formed by DNA with synthetic cationic amphiphiles
for gene transfer have been published.[13±16] A wide variety
of cationic lipids/liposomes with different molecular archi-
tecture have been used for gene delivery. The gene transfer
activity depends on the lipid/DNA ratio and the chemical
composition (e.g., the topology and the head group charge
density) of the lipids, which in turn control the structures of
the DNA±surfactant complexes. Very recently, many re-
search groups have been attracted to this fascinating fron-
tier to study the assembly structures of the DNA±surfac-
tant complexes at a molecular level.[17±25] The use of
modern physical techniques, such as synchrotron small-an-
gle X-ray scattering (SAXS), offers a new look at the su-
pramolecular arrangements in these complexes.
Taking into account the wide scope covered by published
reviews,[1±8,13±16] this review will focus mainly on the current
work of colloidal polyelectrolyte±surfactant complexes and
polyelectrolyte-gel±surfactant complexes. Nanostructures
of the polyelectrolyte±surfactant complexes will be sum-
marized. Physicochemical studies of DNA±surfactant com-
plexes will also be summarized due to their importance in
designing and optimizing strategies for gene therapy.

2. Preparation of Polyelectrolyte±Surfactant
Complexes
2.1. Simple Mixing
Basically, when a polyelectrolyte solution is added to an
oppositely charged surfactant solution or vice versa, poly-
electrolyte±surfactant complexes, as driven by both electro-
static and hydrophobic interactions, will be formed sponta-
neously. The complexation occurs at concentrations much
below the critical micelle concentration (CMC) of the sur-
factant in the external phase. The binding process of surfac-
tants to polyelectrolyte chains is highly cooperative. De-
pending
on
the
charge±molar
ratio
of
surfactant
to
polyelectrolyte, the complexes can be non-stoichiometric
or stoichiometric. The non-stoichiometric complexes con-
taining excess surfactant molecules or charge sites of poly-
electrolyte chains are generally soluble in water. The for-
mation,
structures
and
properties
of
these
types
of
complexes have been studied in detail.[1,26] Near the stoi-
chiometric charge ratio, the complexes are insoluble in
water. The precipitated complexes can be easily isolated,
purified, and dried to form solid materials. However, the
complexes in the water-equilibrated precipitations and in
the completely dried solid state show very different struc-
tures.
2.2. Slow Diffusion of Surfactant to Polyelectrolyte
Network Chains
The preparation of polyelectrolyte-gel±surfactant com-
plexes is usually different from that of linear polyelectro-
lyte±surfactant complexes. Figure 1 shows a typical prepa-
ration
process
of
the
complexes
formed
by
thin
polyelectrolyte gel discs with oppositely charged surfac-
tants. The bulk polyelectrolyte gels are immersed in a very
dilute surfactant solution with concentration a few times
below the CMC value of the surfactant. The complexes are
formed by a very slow diffusion process of surfactant mole-
cules into the polyelectrolyte gels. The polyelectrolyte gels
undergo a volume contraction due to charge neutralization
by the oppositely charged surfactant. Depending on the
charge density and the crosslinker density of the polyelec-
trolyte gel, the surfactant solution concentration, and the
nature of the surfactants and of the polyelectrolyte gel, the
gel can show various degrees of collapse and different col-
lapse kinetics, ranging from a few days to a few months.
Most have a size dependence. If the total charge number of
the surfactant in the initial solution is higher than the
amount of the charge sites of the polyelectrolyte gel, the
complexation is essentially a stoichiometric binding reac-
tion. The small counter ions originally paired with the poly-
electrolyte chains will be exchanged and moved to the out-

Benjamin Chu obtained his B.S. magna cum laude degree from St. Norbert College and his
Ph.D. in physical chemistry from Cornell University. He was a postdoctoral student with the
late Professor Peter J. W. Debye for four years before he started his academic career at the
University of Kansas. In 1968 he moved to the State University of New York at Stony Brook
where he is now a Distinguished Professor.
Shuiqin Zhou received her B.S. and M.S. degrees from the Department of Chemistry, Xiamen
University, PR China. She then moved to the Chinese University of Hong Kong and was
awarded her Ph.D. in 1996. Since 1996 she has been working as a postdoctoral fellow with
Professor Benjamin Chu at the State University of New York at Stony Brook. Her research in-
terests are on polyelectrolyte±surfactant complexes, polymeric gels, assembling of amphiphilic
copolymers, polymer chain collapse, and preparation of nanoparticles and nanocomposites,
including the use of supercritical fluids.

structures can be formed inside the complexes. For exam-
ple, the complexes formed by slightly crosslinked poly(dial-
lyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDADMACl) gels with
sodium dodecyl sulfate (SC12S with 12 being the carbon
atom number of the alkyl chain in the surfactant, the first S
denoting sodium and the second S denoting sulfate accord-
ing to convention) showed such a surfactant concentration
dependence. The collapsed PDADMACl gel±SC12S com-
plex formed in dilute SC12S solution with concentrations
below the CMC showed a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal
structure, while the complexes formed in concentrated
SC12S solution with concentrations above the CMC did not
collapse completely and did not show a well-defined struc-
ture.[27] It should be mentioned that not all of the polyelec-
trolyte-gel±surfactant complexes followed the same surfac-
tant concentration dependence. The complexes formed by
slightly crosslinked poly(sodium methacrylate) (PMANa)
gels with dodecyltrimethylammonium bromide (C12TAB)
always showed a highly ordered Pm3n cubic structure in a
wide range of C12TAB concentrations up to 1.5 M.[28]
3. Colloidal Polyelectrolyte±Surfactant
usually precipitate from aqueous solution. However, Vi-
netsky and Magdassi found that colloidal particles of gela-
tin A±C12TAB and ±C16TAB complexes formed when the
surfactant concentrations were above the CMC.[29] Re-
cently, Kabanov and coworkers[30±33] started to explore new
complexes formed by block or graft polyelectrolytes with
oppositely charged surfactants, in which the blocked or
grafted segments could be either hydrophilic or lipophilic.
Thus, this type of complex represents a special class of lyo-
philic colloids that exhibit combined properties of amphi-
philic copolymers and polyelectrolyte complexes. The com-
plexes formed by poly(ethylene oxide)-block-poly(sodium
methacrylate) (PEO-b-PMANa) and N-alkylpyridinium
bromides (CnPyB with n being the carbon atom number of
the alkyl chain in the surfactant) remain soluble in water in
the whole molar ratio range of [CnPyB]/[PMANa].[30] With
an excess of CnPyB surfactants added to the solution, pos-
itively charged, monodispersed particles were formed. In
addition, they confirmed that narrowly dispersed vesicles
could be formed spontaneously in water by the interactions
of PEO-b-PMANa block polyelectrolytes with single-tail
surfactants of CnPyB and alkyltrimethylammonium bro-
mides (CnTAB).[31] This unique self-assembly behavior
makes the systems promising for use in pharmaceutics such
as drug/gene delivery. However, the complexes formed by
graft copolymer of PEO and poly(ethyleneimine) (PEO-g-
PEI) with oppositely charged sodium alkyl sulfate (SCnS)
in water only showed spherical micelle-like structures with
complex and a hydrophilic shell from PEO chains.[32] The
self-assembly behavior of stoichiometric complexes from
poly(a-methylstyrene)-block-poly(N-ethyl-4-vinylpyridi-
nium) cations (PMeS-b-PE4VP) and bis(2-ethylhexyl) sul-
fonsuccinate (AOT) anions in a variety of low polar organ-
ic solvents were also investigated.[33] Another morphology
of reverse micelle-like colloidal particles was observed,
wherein the block of complex of PE4VP bound with AOT
formed the corona, while the insoluble block of PMeS
formed the core. Figure 2 shows schematic diagrams of pos-
sible assembly architectures of colloidal particles formed by
block or graft polyelectrolyte±surfactant complexes. Ob-
viously, the opportunity for control of the morphology of
broad, and depends on the copolymer architecture (e.g.,

change in charge density of the polyelectrolyte network

4.2. Effects of Gel Crosslinker Density
The crosslinkage of polyelectrolyte chains has significant
effects on the binding process of the surfactant to the
charged network.[58] In a low crosslinker density range of
0.5±2.0 mol.-%, the degree of crosslink had no effect on
the 2D hexagonal structure in the PDADMACl gel±SC12S
complexes.[27] However, the small mesh size in a highly
crosslinked polyelectrolyte gel could hinder the ordered ar-
rangement of the bound surfactants. Thus, no well-defined
structure could be determined. Figure 6 shows the cross-
linker density effects on the SAXS results of fully charged
PMANa gel±C12TAB complexes. The sharp scattering
peaks obtained at 1.0 mol.-% crosslinker density clearly in-
dicated a Pm3n cubic structure. When the crosslinker den-
sity was increased to 4.0 mol.-%, only the three strongest
peaks could be distinguished. Further increasing the cross-
linker density to 7.0 mol.-%, only one broad peak was ob-
served. A similar deteriorating effect of high crosslinker
density on the lamellar structure in the complexes of
poly(4-vinylpyridine) gel±pentadecylphenol was also re-
4.3. Effects of Polyelectrolyte Chain Hydrophobicity
The hydrophobicity of polyelectrolyte chains could be
well-controlled by copolymerizing some hydrophobic seg-
ments to the backbone or side chains. De Oliveira et al.[60]
selected ethyl methacrylate (EMA) as the hydrophobic co-
monomer of poly(acrylic acid). The interactions of CnTAB
with the hydrophobically modified copolymers of P(ANa-
co-EMA) showed that an increase of the hydrophobic
EMA in the copolymer enhanced the aggregation ability of
the surfactants. Philippova et al.[61] copolymerized n-alkyl
acrylates (n = 8, 12, and 18) into PANa gels to modify the
hydrophobicity. The interactions of such a series of copoly-
mer gels with C16PyCl and SDBS surfactants were studied.
The uptake of anionic surfactants to the anionic copolymer
gels could only be completed by strong hydrophobic inter-
actions that induced the swelling of the copolymer gels. In
a different way, we chose two fully charged polyelectrolyte
gels of PMANa and PANa with the same crosslinker den-
sity but different hydrophobicity and chain stiffness in the
backbone chains to complex with the same C10TAB surfac-
tant. The only difference between the two complexes was
the composition of the polyelectrolyte network chains,
namely, PMANa chains were more hydrophobic and stiffer
than PANa chains due to the presence of a methyl group

4.4. Effects of Polyelectrolyte Chain Flexibility
Since the complex is formed by binding surfactant ions
to the polyelectrolyte chains, the shape of aggregates from
the bound surfactants will significantly depend on the chain
flexibility of the polyelectrolyte. Ciferri described the pos-
sible assembly models of surfactant ions bound by a flex-
ible polyelectrolyte chain/network, and a rigid polyelectro-
lyte.[62] The micellization on flexible chains is that of
surfactant clusters forming small beads or short rods with
polyelectrolyte chain loops enclosing the surfactants, while
for relatively rigid polyelectrolyte chains, disk-like or cy-
lindrical micelles might be formed, resulting in an interest-
ing variety of assemblies in the complexes. Figure 7 shows
a schematic of the assembly of bound surfactants to a flex-

models have been supported by some experimental evi-

a structure of hcpc containing only structural element of cy-
linders because of the relative rigidity of PSS and PDAD-
rings.[48,51] However, the complexes of PMANa gel±CnTAB
(n = 10±18) and PMAPS gel±CnPyCl (n = 10±18) formed a
Pm3n cubic structure containing structural elements of
both spheres and short rods due to the relative flexibility of
4.5. Effects of Alkyl Chain Length of the Surfactant
The alkyl chain length of the surfactant not only affects
the hydrophobic interactions of surfactants and polyelec-
trolyte backbone chains, but also the interfacial curvature
of surfactant ions. Thus, by varying the alkyl chain length
of the surfactant, the micellar arrangement of the bound
surfactant can be different from that in the external solu-
tion phase. Usually, no well-defined ordered structures
could be formed when the surfactants with short alkyl
chains (e.g., n £ 8) were used to complex with polyelectro-
lyte gels due to the weak hydrophobic interactions.[44,48,51]
In addition, the decrease in the alkyl chain length of
the surfactant can also induce a structural transition of
the polyelectrolyte-gel±surfactant complexes.[48,51] In the
PDADMACl gel±SCnS complexes, a 2D hexagonal colum-
nar structure with a structural element of cylinders at n
³ 12 was transformed to a Pm3n cubic structure containing
both structural elements of short cylinders and spheres at n
£ 11. While in the P(MANa-co-NIPAM) gel±CnTAB com-
plexes with a charge content of 67 mol.-% in the gel net-
work chains, a decrease in the alkyl chain length of CnTAB
led to the structural transition from a Pm3n cubic structure
at n = 18 to a fcc structure at n = 14±16, and to a hcps struc-
ture at n = 12, in which only a spherical structural element
was enclosed. These structural transitions have been attrib-
uted to the increase in the interfacial curvature of the sur-
factant with a decrease in the surfactant tail length, and
thus, spherical micelles are favorably formed inside the
polyelectrolyte network chains.

The ordered polyelectrolyte-gel±surfactant complexes
have also been exploited as a good reaction matrix to
synthesize metal nanoparticles and inorganic/organic nano-
composites. Bronstein et al.[66±68] have studied the interac-
tions of Pt, Ag, Ni, and Au metal ions with the PMANa
gel±C16PyCl, PDADMACl gel±SC12S, and PDADMACl
gel±SDBS complexes, respectively. After reduction, various
colloidal metal nanoparticles embedded in the body of the
gel±surfactant complexes were formed. Depending on the
structure and properties of the gel±surfactant complexes
and the type of reducing agent, the sizeÐand size distribu-
tionÐof metal nanoparticles could be controlled. Narrowly
distributed Pt and Au particles with a radius of around
2 nm have been prepared inside the PAADMACl gel±
SC12S complex.[68] In comparison with the metal particles
prepared in the PDADMACl gel without the surfactant,
which showed slow aggregation with time, the metal parti-
cles prepared in the ordered PDADMACl gel±SC12S com-
plex were quite stable. This suggested that the ordering in
the collapsed gel±surfactant complex could prevent the ag-
gregation of the prepared metal colloids.

Cationic lipids, composed of a cationic moiety and a lipo-
philic tail, are widely used non-viral transfection reagents
both in cell culture and in animals because of their dual
ability of condensing DNA and fusing or destabilizing cell
membranes.[14±16,82] The critical molecular design parame-
ters of lipids, such as head-group size, lipophilic segments,
and thermal state of the membranous surface, should
change the molecular packing structures inside DNA±cat-
ionic lipid complexes, thus helping in the design of more ef-
fective transfection reagents. It is conceivable that biologi-
cal activity is intimately related to the nature of the DNA±
surfactant complexes whose structure, in turn, is controlled
mainly by the hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions as
well as hydrogen bonding and molecular architecture.
More importantly, the ability to change the design parame-
ters using lipids and lipitoids is precisely the reason that
such biological systems have an edge over synthetic poly-
mers in correlating experiments on complex formation with
further developments in theoretical interpretations.

Experimental results have shown some general trends in
the relationship between the structure of the cationic lipid
and the transfection activity.[15,16] For example, single-
chained cationic lipids such as CnTAB are more cytotoxic
and less efficient than their double-chained counterparts,
while cationic lipids with multivalent head groups have
greater efficiency than do lipids with a monovalent head
group. The structures of the cationic liposome±DNA com-
plexes vary with the lipid composition. Different self-as-
sembled structures with spherical, rod-like and torroidal
shapes have been determined by electron microscopy.[83]
Only some special structures formed under defined condi-
tions showed high transfection activity. However, the detail
structures of DNA±surfactant complexes at the molecular
assembly level have not been widely investigated.
Very recently, synchrotron SAXS has been used to study
the self-assembled structures of the globule particles of
DNA±cationic liposome complexes with size of a few hun-
dred nanometers in 1 wt.-% aqueous solutions.[18,19] A mul-
ti-lamellar structure (La) with alternating lipid bilayer and
DNA monolayers was firstly revealed. Later, a 2D hex-
agonal columnar structure (HII)Ðconsisting of DNA coat-
ed by cationic lipid monolayers and arranged on a hex-
agonal latticeÐwas derived from synchrotron SAXS. The
structural transition from the lamellar to the columnar in-
verted hexagonal could be induced by two pathways:
1) driving the natural curvature of the cationic lipid mono-
layer (C0) to negative by the addition of the neutral helper-
lipid, and 2) reducing the membrane-bending rigidity (k)
by the addition of neutral helper-lipids and the cosurfactant
of hexanol. Figure 10 shows the schematics of the two dis-
tinct pathways to induce the structural transition from La
to HII for the DNA±cationic liposome complexes. It has
been found that the self-assembled structures of the formed
complexes could be correlated to the transfection activity.
For example, the 2D hexagonal columnar phase is signifi-
cantly more efficient at transfecting mammalian cells in
culture in comparison with the lamellar structure of DNA±
cationic liposome complexes. Optical microscopy revealed
that the DNA±cationic liposome complexes with multi-la-
mellar structure could bind stably to anionic vesicles (mod-
els of cellular membranes), while complexes with 2D hex-
agonal columnar structure were unstable and could rapidly
fuse and release DNA when contacting with anionic vesi-
cles. Accordingly, it will be essential to clarify the nano-
structures of the DNA±cationic surfactant complexes in or-
der to understand the structure±activity relationship. On
the basis of these experimentally observed nanostructures
of lamellar, hexagonal and spaghetti-like organizations, a
few groups have been involved in developing (still primi-
tive) theoretical models to explain the interactions and
structures in DNA±surfactant complexes.[20±24]
The DNA±cationic liposome complexation was also af-

ionic moieties of the lipids.[14±16] The complexes formed in
different buffer solution medium showed large differences

By using the copolyelectrolytes with special architectures
of graft or block, branched or linear, variable segment
length and hydrophobic properties, lyophilic colloidal par-
ticles of stoichiometric polyelectrolyte±surfactant com-
plexes could be prepared with very rich morphology. This
special class of colloids exhibits combined properties of
amphiphilic copolymers and polyelectrolyte complexes and
can be exploited in a wide range of technologies such as
water purification, drug delivery, cosmetics, and food
science. With very slow complexation processes, polyelec-
trolyte-gel±surfactant complexes could exhibit highly or-
dered structures. Besides the commonly observed layered
structure, a series of assembled structures of Pm3n cubic,
2D hexagonal close packing of cylinders, hexagonal close
packing of spheres, face-centered cubic, and Ia3d cubic
have been observed in polyelectrolyte-gel±surfactant com-
plexes, depending on various properties of polyelectrolyte
chains and surfactants. Lamellar structure is still the pre-
dominant arrangement in the solid-state polyelectrolyte±
surfactant complexes. By selecting polyelectrolytes or sur-
factant molecules with special properties, such as low sur-
face energy, photosensitivity, thermosensitivity, and high
conductivity, these highly ordered polyelectrolyte (gel)±
surfactant complexes represent a new class of materials
with important industrial and biological applications.
Although a tremendous amount of research papers on
polyelectrolyte±surfactant complexes has been published
in the last fifteen years, the building of various highly or-
dered structures in complexes with special mechanical,
electrostatic, optical, and biological properties remains an
exciting field. The modification of molecular architecture
of polyelectrolyte chains and surfactants may be of future
interest for exploring more assembly structures and func-
tions of the polyelectrolyte±surfactant complexes. The
highly ordered solid-like water-containing polyelectrolyte-
gel±surfactant complexes may function as microreactors
for inorganic and biological reactions. The DNA±cationic
lipid complexes used in gene therapy are a special type of
polyelectrolyte±surfactant complex. The structural study of
DNA±lipid complexes at the molecular assembly level is
very important for understanding the structure±activity re-
lationship and should remain a hot topic.
